name: Sand Springs
short_description: BLM public land near Fallon offering free rockhounding for agate, chalcedony, and jasper with no permit required. A beginner-friendly desert site perfect for family collecting.
description:
Sand Springs is a public BLM rockhounding location in Churchill County, Nevada, situated in the high desert basin and range terrain east of Fallon. The site sits at 4756 feet elevation on moderate slopes typical of Nevada's mineral-rich desert landscape. This area is known for producing colorful agate nodules, chalcedony specimens, and jasper fragments scattered across washes and exposed hillsides.

The geology here reflects the Basin and Range province's extensive Tertiary-age volcanic activity. Rhyolitic and basaltic eruptions, combined with hot spring and geothermal silica deposition, created ideal conditions for microcrystalline quartz minerals like agate and chalcedony to form within fractures and cavities. The modern landscape of sand, gravel, and exposed volcanic outcrops makes these deposits accessible and visible on the surface.

Collectors visit Sand Springs year-round but find the best conditions in cooler months when desert heat is manageable. The site requires no permit and welcomes recreational rockhounders under standard BLM personal-use rules. Most specimens are found in dry washes and along gentle hillsides, making it suitable for families and beginners willing to explore moderate terrain.

Expect to spend several hours searching across multiple small areas. Bring sun protection and plenty of water, as the high desert offers little shade. The collecting experience here is relaxed and low-pressure - ideal for learning to spot agate and chalcedony in their natural setting.
history: Sand Springs lies within the Basin and Range physiographic province, where Miocene and Pliocene volcanic activity (roughly 23 to 3 million years ago) deposited thick sequences of rhyolitic ash flows and basalt. Subsequent geothermal circulation and silica-rich hot spring activity infilled fractures with microcrystalline quartz, forming the agate nodules and chalcedony deposits found here today. The area has long been known to local rockhounders as a reliable source for colorful specimens.
tips: Search dry washes and the base of gentle hillsides where erosion naturally exposes agate nodules and chalcedony fragments - look for smooth, rounded stones in tan, brown, red, and white colors. Bring a 10x loupe to check for the fine-grained, waxy luster typical of quality agate; specimens here often show banding or moss-like patterns. A rock hammer is helpful for testing hardness and splitting thin-shell agates, but most surface-collected specimens require only picking up. Collect in early morning to avoid midday heat and to see translucent specimens backlit by the sun.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4756 feet in high desert with moderate slopes. Terrain is predominantly sand, gravel, and exposed volcanic rock with sparse low scrub vegetation. Walking distances from parking vary from 50 yards to over a mile depending on where collectors choose to explore. Footing is uneven but not technically difficult - rocky ground and occasional loose scree require sturdy hiking shoes. No shade available.
best_season: October - April. The high desert climate makes summer (May-September) extremely hot, often exceeding 90-100 degrees F with midday temperatures dangerous for extended collecting. Fall through spring offers comfortable 50-70 degree F conditions and clearer visibility of translucent specimens.
written_directions: From Fallon, take US 95 north approximately 25 miles toward Gerlach. Look for unmarked BLM land access on the east side of the highway near the Churchill County area. The GPS coordinates 39.269026, -118.348339 mark the site. Park at any safe pullout along local roads or designated BLM access points. Consult a current NV BLM map for exact road access, as routes in this region are often unimproved and conditions vary seasonally.
road_conditions: Unpaved roads may be washboard or rough depending on season and recent maintenance. High-clearance passenger vehicles recommended; avoid during or immediately after rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and BLM access points. No developed parking lot. Typically space for 2-4 vehicles at any given spot. Arrive early during cooler months when site attracts more visitors.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No power tools. Collect only on BLM land; respect posted private property boundaries. Pack out all trash. Leave the site as you found it.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection allowed
No motorized excavation or power tools
Respect private property boundaries
Pack out all trash
Leave the site undisturbed
nearest_services: Fallon, approximately 25 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved roads to parking areas. Uneven rocky ground and occasional loose scree require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access. Minimal shade or water sources on-site.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Sand Springs, Nevada?
faq.answer: Sand Springs is known for agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. Most collectors find colorful agate nodules in tan, brown, red, and white with banding or moss patterns. Chalcedony fragments are also common. Surface collecting along washes typically yields the best results.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Sand Springs near Fallon?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Sand Springs is on public BLM land open to recreational rockhounding under FLPMA personal-use guidelines. You can collect free of charge up to 25 lbs per day with no advance registration needed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Sand Springs, NV?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer temperatures in this high desert location often exceed 90-100 degrees F, making daytime collecting dangerous. Fall, winter, and spring offer comfortable 50-70 degree conditions and better visibility for spotting translucent agate specimens.

faq.question: Is Sand Springs rockhounding site family-friendly and suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Sand Springs is excellent for families and beginners. The terrain is relatively easy with gentle slopes and no technical climbing required. No permit or special knowledge is needed. Kids ages 6 and up can participate. The relaxed, open landscape makes it ideal for learning to identify agate and chalcedony in natural settings.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound at Sand Springs, Churchill County?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking shoes for uneven rocky ground, a 10x loupe to examine fine detail and banding in agate, and a small rock hammer for testing hardness. Most importantly, bring multiple liters of water and sun protection (hat, sunscreen, sunglasses), as the high desert offers no shade. Carrying bags or buckets help transport your 25-lb daily limit.

faq.question: How far is Sand Springs rockhounding area from Fallon, Nevada?
faq.answer: Sand Springs is approximately 25 miles north of Fallon via US 95. The drive takes about 35-40 minutes from town. Fallon offers all services including gas, food, and lodging. Cell service at the site is spotty, so download maps beforehand and inform someone of your location.

meta_description: Free rockhounding on BLM land near Fallon, NV. Find agate, chalcedony, and jasper. No permit required. Perfect for families and beginners.
